title: Human-Computer Interaction Fundamentals
description: Learn core HCI principles, user research methods, and design practices for creating intuitive digital experiences.

nodes.title: Introduction to HCI Principles
nodes.content: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is the study of how people interact with computers and digital systems. Core principles include: **Visibility** (users can see system state), **Feedback** (system responds to user actions), **Constraints** (design prevents errors), and **Consistency** (similar actions produce similar results). Don Norman's design principles emphasize making systems intuitive by aligning with user expectations and mental models. Good HCI design reduces cognitive load and makes powerful features accessible to all users.

nodes.title: Real-World HCI: The iPhone Interface
nodes.content: When Apple's iPhone launched, it revolutionized HCI by introducing the touchscreen and gesture-based interaction. The home screen uses visual affordances (icons look pressable), immediate feedback (haptic vibration when tapping), and consistent interactions (swiping always means scrolling/navigating). The simple interface succeeded because it followed core HCI principles: users instantly understood what was clickable, received immediate feedback, and could predict behavior based on prior actions. This user-centered design made smartphones accessible to millions.

nodes.title: Assessment: HCI Principles
nodes.content: Test your understanding of HCI principles and real-world applications.

questions.question: Which HCI principle is demonstrated when a button changes color when you hover over it?
feedback: No, visibility is about seeing system state.
Correct! The color change provides immediate feedback to the user's action.
No, constraints prevent errors.
No, consistency is about predictable behavior.
questions.explanation: Feedback is when the system responds to user input. Changing the button's appearance shows the user that the system registered their hover action.

questions.question: A designer prevents users from deleting their account without confirming their password first. What HCI principle is this?
feedback: No, this isn't just about feedback.
No, this isn't about consistent interactions.
Correct! This constraint prevents the error of accidentally deleting an account.
No, this isn't about seeing system state.
questions.explanation: Constraints are design features that prevent errors. Requiring confirmation reduces mistakes and protects important user data.

nodes.title: User Research and Usability Testing
nodes.content: To design effective interfaces, HCI professionals must understand real users through research methods: **Interviews** reveal user goals and pain points through open conversation. **Observations** (contextual inquiry) show how people actually use systems in natural settings. **Surveys** gather quantitative data from many users. **Usability Testing** involves watching users complete tasks to identify confusing elements. These methods uncover gaps between designer assumptions and user reality. Data-driven design based on user research significantly improves interface intuitiveness and user satisfaction.

nodes.title: A/B Testing for Better Design
nodes.content: An e-commerce company notices users frequently abandon their shopping carts. Through usability testing, designers discover the checkout process has 4 confusing pages. They create a simpler single-page checkout (version B) and test it with 50% of users. After two weeks, version B reduces cart abandonment by 23% and increases conversion rate. This data-driven approach proves that removing unnecessary complexity directly improves user behavior—a key HCI insight. Designers now roll out the single-page checkout to all users based on real evidence, not assumptions.

nodes.title: Assessment: User Research Methods
nodes.content: Test your understanding of user research and usability testing approaches.

questions.question: A UX researcher wants to understand how customers currently use a banking app in their daily lives. Which research method is best?
feedback: Surveys gather numbers but miss context.
Correct! Contextual inquiry directly observes real behavior in natural settings, revealing actual usage patterns.
The design team has assumptions, not user insights.
Competitors don't represent your users.
questions.explanation: Contextual inquiry (observation) is ideal for understanding real-world behavior. It shows *how* and *why* users interact with the system, not just *what* they think.

questions.question: During usability testing, a user clicks repeatedly on a feature thinking it's a button, but it's actually just an image. What does this reveal?
options: The interface has poor affordance design
feedback: User confusion indicates a design problem, not a training problem.
Correct! Affordance is the visual clue that something is interactive. The image should look clickable to prevent this confusion.
Usability issues affect all users.
Usefulness and clickability are different problems.
questions.explanation: Affordance in HCI means the interface clearly shows what is (and isn't) interactive. Visual cues like buttons, underlines, and color indicate clickability.

nodes.title: What Would You Like to Learn Next?
nodes.content: You've mastered HCI principles! Choose your next learning path:

choices.label: Learn about accessibility and inclusive design

nodes.title: Practice: HCI Fundamentals
nodes.content: Let's reinforce your understanding with additional practice questions.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key benefit of usability testing over design reviews?
options: Real users reveal unexpected problems designers miss
feedback: Actually usability testing takes more time.
Correct! Real user behavior often contradicts designer assumptions.
Usability testing requires participants and space.
No design method guarantees perfection.
questions.explanation: The power of usability testing is that it shows *actual user behavior* rather than designer opinions. This gap between assumptions and reality is where real insights come from.

questions.question: A website displays error messages in light gray text on a white background. Users frequently miss the error message. Which HCI principle is violated?
feedback: The feedback exists but isn't perceived.
Correct! Visibility means users must be able to see important information. Low contrast violates this principle.
Accessibility includes visibility, but the core principle here is visibility.
Metaphor isn't about visual clarity.
questions.explanation: Visibility means making critical information perceptible. Error messages that blend into the background fail this principle and frustrate users.

nodes.title: Congratulations!
nodes.content: You've completed the HCI Fundamentals course! You now understand core principles like feedback and constraints, real-world HCI design patterns, and the importance of user research methods. These foundations will help you evaluate digital interfaces critically and contribute to user-centered design in any role. **Next steps:** Apply these principles by analyzing interfaces you use daily, conduct simple usability tests with friends, or explore specialized topics like accessibility, interaction design, or information architecture.
